content.problemStatement:
Chefs from all over the globe gather each year for an international convention. Each chef represents some country. Please, note that more than one chef can represent a country.

Each of them presents their best dish to the audience. The audience then sends emails to a secret and secure mail server, with the subject being the name of the chef whom they wish to elect as the **"Chef of the Year"**.

You will be given the list of the subjects of all the emails. Find the country whose chefs got the most number of votes, and also the chef who got elected as the **"Chef of the Year"** (the chef who got the most number of votes).

**Note 1**

If several countries got the maximal number of votes, consider the country with the lexicographically smaller name among them to be a winner. Similarly if several chefs got the maximal number of votes, consider the chef with the lexicographically smaller name among them to be a winner.

**Note 2**

The string **A** = **a1a2...an** is called lexicographically smaller then the string **B** = **b1b2...bm** in the following two cases:

there exists index **i** ≤ **min{n, m}** such that **aj** = **bj** for **1 ≤ j < i** and **ai** < **bi**;
**A** is a proper prefix of **B**, that is, **n < m** and **aj** = **bj** for **1 ≤ j ≤ n**.The characters in strings are compared by their ASCII codes.

Refer to function **strcmp** in C or to standard comparator **<** for **string** data structure in C++ for details.
constraints: Each string in the input contains only letters of English alphabets (uppercase or lowercase)
Each string in the input has length not exceeding 10
All chef names will be distinct
Subject of each email will coincide with the name of one of the chefs
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers N and M, where N is the number of chefs and M is the number of emails. Each of the following N lines contains two space-separated strings, the name of the chef and their country. Each of the following M lines contains one string, the subject of the email.
content.outputFormat: The output should consist of two lines. The first line should contain the name of the country whose chefs got the most number of votes. The second line should contain the name of the chef who is elected as the "Chef of the Year".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Here we have only one chef Leibniz and he is from Germany. Clearly, all votes are for him. So Germany is the country-winner and Leibniz is the "Chef of the Year".

sampleTestCases.input:
4 5
Ramanujan India
Torricelli Italy
Gauss Germany
Lagrange Italy
Ramanujan
Torricelli
Torricelli
Ramanujan
Lagrange
sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Here we have chefs Torricelli and Lagrange from Italy, chef Ramanujan from India and chef Gauss from Germany. Torricelli got 2 votes, while Lagrange got one vote. Hence the Italy got 3 votes in all. Ramanujan got also 2 votes. And so India got 2 votes in all. Finally Gauss got no votes leaving Germany without votes. So the country-winner is Italy without any ties. But we have two chefs with 2 votes: Torricelli and Ramanujan. But since the string "Ramanujan" is lexicographically smaller than "Torricelli", then Ramanujan is the "Chef of the Year".

sampleTestCases.input:
2 2
Newton England
Euclid Greece
Newton
Euclid
sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Here we have two countries with 1 vote: England and Greece. Since the string "England" is lexicographically smaller than "Greece", then England is the country-winner. Next, we have two chefs with 1 vote: Newton and Euclid. Since the string "Euclid" is lexicographically smaller than "Newton", then Euclid is the "Chef of the Year".

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a hashing approach to store the country of each chef and the number of votes for each chef. We then iterate over the emails to update the vote count for each chef. After that, we find the country with the maximum total votes and the chef with the maximum votes. If there are multiple countries or chefs with the same maximum votes, we choose the one with the lexicographically smaller name.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, O(T * (N + M)) total. The hashing and string comparison operations take constant time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — we store the country and vote count for each chef.
